core_behavior: The system must automatically log out a user after a specified period of inactivity, known as the session timeout period.
The system will monitor user activity and track the time elapsed since the last user interaction.
When the session timeout period is reached, the system will terminate the user's session, removing access to protected resources and functionality.
inputs: User interactions (e.g., mouse clicks, keyboard input, page navigation) to track activity
Session timeout period (a configurable value, e.g., 30 minutes)
User authentication information (e.g., username, password, session ID) to identify the logged-in user
outputs: A logout response, redirecting the user to the login page or a designated logout page
An updated session state, reflecting the terminated session
Optional: a notification or warning to the user before the session timeout period is reached (e.g., a pop-up message or a countdown timer)
assumptions: The system uses a server-side session management mechanism (e.g., cookies, session IDs) to track user sessions.
The session timeout period is configurable by the system administrator.
The system has a defined login and logout process, with associated pages or endpoints.
The system will use a reasonable default value for the session timeout period if no value is explicitly configured (e.g., 30 minutes).
The system will not distinguish between different types of user interactions (e.g., all interactions will be treated equally in terms of resetting the session timeout timer).
failure_modes: If the system fails to detect user inactivity, the session may not timeout as expected, potentially allowing unauthorized access to protected resources.
If the system fails to terminate the user's session, the user may remain logged in indefinitely, potentially causing security issues.
If the system experiences a failure during the logout process, the user's session may not be properly terminated, leading to inconsistent state.
edge_cases: A user has multiple browser tabs or windows open, and activity in one tab/window does not affect the session timeout timer in other tabs/windows.
A user is engaged in a long-running operation (e.g., a file upload or download), and the session timeout timer should be reset or paused to prevent interruption.
A user has a slow or unreliable network connection, which may cause delays in detecting user activity or terminating the session.
A user intentionally attempts to bypass or manipulate the session timeout mechanism (e.g., using automated scripts or tools).
security_constraints: The system must ensure that terminated sessions are properly removed from the system, preventing unauthorized access to protected resources.
The system must use secure protocols (e.g., HTTPS) to protect user authentication information and session data.
The system must implement measures to prevent session fixation attacks (e.g., regenerating session IDs after login).
The system must comply with relevant security standards and regulations (e.g., OWASP, PCI-DSS).
performance_constraints: The system must be able to handle a large number of concurrent user sessions without significant performance degradation.
The system must be able to detect user inactivity and terminate sessions in a timely manner, without introducing noticeable delays or latency.
The system must minimize the overhead associated with session management, to ensure optimal system performance and responsiveness.
The system must be able to scale to accommodate increasing numbers of users and sessions, without compromising performance or security.
